explanations.8: This variable gets replaced with the actual path of your local workspace folder on your computer when the dev container starts.
explanations.13: Setting this to 'true' allows the non-root user (vscode) to run Docker commands inside the container without needing admin privileges.
explanations.14: This uses Moby (an open-source project that Docker is built on) instead of the full Docker engine, which is lighter weight for running Docker inside Docker.
explanations.19: This command runs once before the container is created—it sets up a directory for bash history and tells git to ignore the history file so it doesn't get committed.
explanations.20: This command runs after you connect to the container and silently installs git pre-commit hooks if the pre-commit tool exists, without failing if it doesn't.
explanations.34: This wraps long lines in the editor, but only at the column number specified on the next line (80 characters).
explanations.62: These numbers are visual guides showing where lines should ideally be kept short—120 and 70 characters are common limits for code readability.
explanations.72: This tells VS Code to treat JSON files in the 'resources' folder as JSONC (JSON with Comments), so you can add comments in those files.
explanations.76: When you save a file, VS Code will use whatever language mode the currently active editor has, rather than guessing based on the filename.
explanations.88: This configures how VS Code should find and fold code regions in Dockerfiles when you use special comments like '# region' and '# endregion'.
explanations.113: This maps file paths to JSON schema URLs—VS Code uses these schemas to validate your YAML files and provide helpful autocompletion.
explanations.120: The title bar will show the project name, a separator, and '[DevContainer]' to remind you that you're working inside a container, plus a dot if there are unsaved changes.
